From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American film director

| Budd Boetticher | |
| --- | --- |
| Born | Oscar Boetticher Jr.  (1916-07-29)July 29, 1916  [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago"), [Illinois](/wiki/Illinois "Illinois"), U.S. |
| Died | November 29, 2001(2001-11-29) (aged 85)  [Ramona, California](/wiki/Ramona,_California "Ramona, California"), U.S. |
| Occupation | Film director |
| Years active | 1942–1985 |
| Spouses | Marian Forsythe Herr  ​  ​ (m. 1938; div. 1946)​      Emily Erskine Cook  ​  ​ (m. 1949; div. 1959)​      [Debra Paget](/wiki/Debra_Paget "Debra Paget")  ​  ​ (m. 1960; div. 1961)​      Margo E. Jensen  ​  ​ (m. 1969; div. 1971)​      Mary Chelde  ​  ​ (m. 1971)​ |

**Oscar Boetticher Jr.** ([/ˈbɛtɪkər/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*BET-i-kər*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"); July 29, 1916 – November 29, 2001), known as **Budd Boetticher**,
was an American film director. He is best remembered for a series of low-budget Westerns he made in the late 1950s starring [Randolph Scott](/wiki/Randolph_Scott "Randolph Scott").[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-los-2)

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Budd_Boetticher&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Boetticher was born in Chicago. His mother died in childbirth, and his father was killed in an accident shortly afterward. He was adopted by a wealthy couple, Oscar Boetticher Sr. (1867–1953) and Georgia ([née](/wiki/Birth_name#Maiden_and_married_names "Birth name") Naas) Boetticher (1888–1955), and raised in [Evansville, Indiana](/wiki/Evansville,_Indiana "Evansville, Indiana"), along with his younger brother, Henry Edward Boetticher (1924–2004). He attended [Culver Military Academy](/wiki/Culver_Military_Academy "Culver Military Academy"), where he became friends with [Hal Roach Jr.](/wiki/Hal_Roach_Jr. "Hal Roach Jr.")[[3]](#cite_note-ax-3)

He was a star athlete at [Ohio State University](/wiki/Ohio_State_University "Ohio State University"), until an injury ended his sports career. In 1939, he traveled to Mexico, where he learned [bullfighting](/wiki/Bullfighting "Bullfighting") under Lorenzo Garza, Fermín Espinosa Saucedo, and [Carlos Arruza](/wiki/Carlos_Arruza "Carlos Arruza").[[2]](#cite_note-los-2)[[3]](#cite_note-ax-3)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Budd_Boetticher&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

### Early films

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Budd_Boetticher&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early films")]

Boetticher worked as a crew member on *[Of Mice and Men](/wiki/Of_Mice_and_Men_(1939_film) "Of Mice and Men (1939 film)")* (1939) and *[A Chump at Oxford](/wiki/A_Chump_at_Oxford "A Chump at Oxford")* (1940). A chance encounter with [Rouben Mamoulian](/wiki/Rouben_Mamoulian "Rouben Mamoulian") landed him a job as technical advisor on *[Blood and Sand](/wiki/Blood_and_Sand_(1941_film) "Blood and Sand (1941 film)")* (1941). He stayed on in Hollywood working at [Hal Roach Studios](/wiki/Hal_Roach_Studios "Hal Roach Studios") doing a variety of jobs.[[3]](#cite_note-ax-3)

### Columbia Pictures

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Budd_Boetticher&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Columbia Pictures")]

Boetticher received an offer to work at [Columbia Pictures](/wiki/Columbia_Pictures "Columbia Pictures") as an assistant director on *[The More the Merrier](/wiki/The_More_the_Merrier "The More the Merrier")* (1943). The studio liked his work, and he stayed to assist on *[Submarine Raider](/wiki/Submarine_Raider "Submarine Raider")* (1942), *[The Desperadoes](/wiki/The_Desperadoes "The Desperadoes")* (1943), *[Destroyer](/wiki/Destroyer_(1943_film) "Destroyer (1943 film)")* (1943), *[U-Boat Prisoner](/wiki/U-Boat_Prisoner "U-Boat Prisoner")* (1944), and *[Cover Girl](/wiki/Cover_Girl_(film) "Cover Girl (film)")* (1944), promoted to first assistant director. Some of these were Columbia's most prestigious films and Boetticher was offered the chance to join the studio's directing program.[[3]](#cite_note-ax-3)

Boetticher's first credited film as director was a [Boston Blackie](/wiki/Boston_Blackie "Boston Blackie") film *[One Mysterious Night](/wiki/One_Mysterious_Night "One Mysterious Night")* (1944). It was followed by other "B" movies: *[The Missing Juror](/wiki/The_Missing_Juror "The Missing Juror")* (1944), *[Youth on Trial](/wiki/Youth_on_Trial "Youth on Trial")* (1945), *[A Guy, a Gal and a Pal](/wiki/A_Guy,_a_Gal_and_a_Pal "A Guy, a Gal and a Pal")* (1945), and *[Escape in the Fog](/wiki/Escape_in_the_Fog "Escape in the Fog")* (1945).[[3]](#cite_note-ax-3)

"They were terrible pictures", he remarked in 1979. "We had eight or ten days to make a picture. We had all these people who later became stars, or didn't, like [George Macready](/wiki/George_Macready "George Macready") and [Nina Foch](/wiki/Nina_Foch "Nina Foch"), and you never had anybody any good. I don't mean that they weren't good but they weren't then, and neither were we."[[3]](#cite_note-ax-3)

### Military service

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Budd_Boetticher&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Military service")]

Boetticher was commissioned as an ensign in the U.S. [Naval Photographic Science Laboratory](/wiki/Naval_Photographic_Science_Laboratory "Naval Photographic Science Laboratory"). He made documentaries and service films, including *[The Fleet That Came to Stay](/wiki/The_Fleet_That_Came_to_Stay "The Fleet That Came to Stay")* (1945) and *Well Done*.[[3]](#cite_note-ax-3)

### Eagle Lion and Monogram

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Budd_Boetticher&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Eagle Lion and Monogram")]

Boetticher left Columbia. He directed some films for [Eagle Lion](/wiki/Eagle-Lion_Films "Eagle-Lion Films"), *[Assigned to Danger](/wiki/Assigned_to_Danger "Assigned to Danger")* (1948) and *[Behind Locked Doors](/wiki/Behind_Locked_Doors "Behind Locked Doors")* (1949).

At [Monogram Pictures](/wiki/Monogram_Pictures "Monogram Pictures") he directed [Roddy McDowall](/wiki/Roddy_McDowall "Roddy McDowall") in *[Black Midnight](/wiki/Black_Midnight_(film) "Black Midnight (film)")* (1949) and *[Killer Shark](/wiki/Killer_Shark "Killer Shark")* (1950). In between, he made *[The Wolf Hunters](/wiki/The_Wolf_Hunters_(1949_film) "The Wolf Hunters (1949 film)")* (1949).

He began directing for television with *[Magnavox Theatre](/wiki/Magnavox_Theatre "Magnavox Theatre")* – a production of *[The Three Musketeers](/wiki/The_Three_Musketeers "The Three Musketeers")* that was released theatrically in some markets as *[The Blade of the Musketeers](/wiki/The_Blade_of_the_Musketeers "The Blade of the Musketeers")*.[[3]](#cite_note-ax-3)

### *Bullfighter and the Lady*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Budd_Boetticher&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Bullfighter and the Lady")]

Boetticher got his first big break when he was asked to direct *[Bullfighter and the Lady](/wiki/Bullfighter_and_the_Lady "Bullfighter and the Lady")* for [John Wayne](/wiki/John_Wayne "John Wayne")'s production company, [Batjac](/wiki/Batjac "Batjac"), based loosely on Boetticher's own adventures studying to be a matador in Mexico. It was the first film he signed as Budd Boetticher, rather than his given name, and it earned him an Oscar nomination for Best Original Story. The film was edited drastically without his consent, though, and his career again seemed on hold.[[4]](#cite_note-4) (The film has since been restored by the [UCLA](/wiki/UCLA "UCLA") Film Archive and the restored print is sometimes referred to by its working title, *Torero*.)[[3]](#cite_note-ax-3)

### Universal-International

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Budd_Boetticher&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Universal-International")]

Boetticher signed a contract to direct for [Universal-International](/wiki/Universal-International "Universal-International"), where he specialized in Westerns.

"I became a Western director because they thought I looked like one and they thought I rode better than anyone else," said Boetticher later. "And I didn't know anything about the West."[[3]](#cite_note-ax-3)

His films there included *[The Cimarron Kid](/wiki/The_Cimarron_Kid "The Cimarron Kid")* (1952) with [Audie Murphy](/wiki/Audie_Murphy "Audie Murphy"); *[Bronco Buster](/wiki/Bronco_Buster_(film) "Bronco Buster (film)")* (1952); *[Red Ball Express](/wiki/Red_Ball_Express_(film) "Red Ball Express (film)")* (1952), a [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") film; *[Horizons West](/wiki/Horizons_West "Horizons West")* (1952) with [Robert Ryan](/wiki/Robert_Ryan "Robert Ryan"); *[City Beneath the Sea](/wiki/City_Beneath_the_Sea_(1953_film) "City Beneath the Sea (1953 film)")* (1953), a treasure-hunting film; *[Seminole](/wiki/Seminole_(film) "Seminole (film)")* (1953), a Western with [Rock Hudson](/wiki/Rock_Hudson "Rock Hudson"); *[The Man from the Alamo](/wiki/The_Man_from_the_Alamo "The Man from the Alamo")* (1953) with [Glenn Ford](/wiki/Glenn_Ford "Glenn Ford"); *[Wings of the Hawk](/wiki/Wings_of_the_Hawk "Wings of the Hawk")* (1953) with [Van Heflin](/wiki/Van_Heflin "Van Heflin"); and *[East of Sumatra](/wiki/East_of_Sumatra "East of Sumatra")* (1953) with Chandler and Quinn.

He started directing *[The Americano](/wiki/The_Americano_(1955_film) "The Americano (1955 film)")*, an independent film with Ford, but quit.[[3]](#cite_note-ax-3) He returned to television with *[The Public Defender](/wiki/The_Public_Defender_(TV_series) "The Public Defender (TV series)")*.

### *The Magnificent Matador*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Budd_Boetticher&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: The Magnificent Matador")]

In 1955, he helmed another bullfighting drama, *[The Magnificent Matador](/wiki/The_Magnificent_Matador "The Magnificent Matador")*, at 20th Century-Fox, which began his frequent collaboration with cinematographer [Lucien Ballard](/wiki/Lucien_Ballard "Lucien Ballard"). They followed it with a film noir, *[The Killer Is Loose](/wiki/The_Killer_Is_Loose "The Killer Is Loose")* (1956).[[3]](#cite_note-ax-3)

He also directed episodes of *[The Count of Monte Cristo](/wiki/The_Count_of_Monte_Cristo_(1956_TV_series) "The Count of Monte Cristo (1956 TV series)")*.

### Ranown Cycle

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Budd_Boetticher&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Ranown Cycle")]

Boetticher finally achieved his major breakthrough when he teamed up with actor [Randolph Scott](/wiki/Randolph_Scott "Randolph Scott") and [screenwriter](/wiki/Screenwriter "Screenwriter") [Burt Kennedy](/wiki/Burt_Kennedy "Burt Kennedy") to make *[Seven Men from Now](/wiki/Seven_Men_from_Now "Seven Men from Now")* (1956). It was the first of the seven films (last in 1960) that came to be known as the Ranown Cycle.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

He was reunited with Scott and Kennedy on *[The Tall T](/wiki/The_Tall_T "The Tall T")* (1957); they were joined by producer [Harry Joe Brown](/wiki/Harry_Joe_Brown "Harry Joe Brown"), who would produce the six remaining films.

Boetticher directed the first three episodes of the TV series *[Maverick](/wiki/Maverick_(TV_series) "Maverick (TV series)")*. He went back to working with Scott: *[Decision at Sundown](/wiki/Decision_at_Sundown "Decision at Sundown")* (1957), *[Buchanan Rides Alone](/wiki/Buchanan_Rides_Alone "Buchanan Rides Alone")* (1958) (not written by Kennedy), and *[Ride Lonesome](/wiki/Ride_Lonesome "Ride Lonesome")* (1959).

*[Westbound](/wiki/Westbound_(film) "Westbound (film)")* (1959) was made with Scott but without Kennedy or Brown. *[Comanche Station](/wiki/Comanche_Station "Comanche Station")* (1960) was made with Scott and Kennedy.

### 1960s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Budd_Boetticher&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: 1960s")]

Boetticher returned to television, directing episodes of *[Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong_(TV_series) "Hong Kong (TV series)")*, *[Dick Powell's Zane Grey Theatre](/wiki/Dick_Powell%27s_Zane_Grey_Theatre "Dick Powell's Zane Grey Theatre")*, *[Death Valley Days](/wiki/Death_Valley_Days "Death Valley Days")*, and *[The Rifleman](/wiki/The_Rifleman "The Rifleman")*. He did a feature, *[The Rise and Fall of Legs Diamond](/wiki/The_Rise_and_Fall_of_Legs_Diamond "The Rise and Fall of Legs Diamond")* (1960). He directed the first three episodes of *[Maverick](/wiki/Maverick_(TV_series) "Maverick (TV series)")* starring [James Garner](/wiki/James_Garner "James Garner") then had a fundamental disagreement with writer/producer [Roy Huggins](/wiki/Roy_Huggins "Roy Huggins") involving the lead character's dialogue and never directed the series again.

Boetticher spent most of the 1960s south of the border pursuing his obsession, the documentary of his friend, the [bullfighter](/wiki/Bullfighter "Bullfighter") [Carlos Arruza](/wiki/Carlos_Arruza "Carlos Arruza"), turning down profitable [Hollywood](/wiki/Cinema_of_the_United_States "Cinema of the United States") offers and suffering humiliation and despair to stay with the project, including sickness, bankruptcy and confinement in both jail and asylum (all of which is detailed in his [autobiography](/wiki/Autobiography "Autobiography") *When in Disgrace*). *[Arruza](/wiki/Arruza_(film) "Arruza (film)")* was finally completed in 1968 and released in Mexico in 1971 and the US in 1972.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[2]](#cite_note-los-2)

### Return to Hollywood

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Budd_Boetticher&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Return to Hollywood")]

Boetticher returned to Hollywood with the rarely seen *[A Time for Dying](/wiki/A_Time_for_Dying "A Time for Dying")*, a collaboration with Audie Murphy shot in 1969 and not released widely until 1982. He provided the story for [Don Siegel](/wiki/Don_Siegel "Don Siegel")'s *[Two Mules for Sister Sara](/wiki/Two_Mules_for_Sister_Sara "Two Mules for Sister Sara")* (1970).[[7]](#cite_note-7)

In his later years, he was known for the [documentary](/wiki/Documentary_film "Documentary film") *[My Kingdom For...](/wiki/My_Kingdom_For... "My Kingdom For...")* (1985) and his appearance as a judge in [Robert Towne](/wiki/Robert_Towne "Robert Towne")'s *[Tequila Sunrise](/wiki/Tequila_Sunrise_(film) "Tequila Sunrise (film)")* (1988), and he was still actively attempting to get his screenplay, *A Horse for Mr. Barnum*, made before his death in 2001. His wife Mary and he spent much of their later years traveling to film festivals around the world, especially in Europe. His last public appearance, less than three months before his death, was at Cinecon, a classic film festival held in Hollywood, California.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Filmography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Budd_Boetticher&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Filmography")]

* *[Of Mice and Men](/wiki/Of_Mice_and_Men_(1939_film) "Of Mice and Men (1939 film)")* (1939) – horse wrangler
* *[A Chump at Oxford](/wiki/A_Chump_at_Oxford "A Chump at Oxford")* (1940)- crew
* *[Blood and Sand](/wiki/Blood_and_Sand_(1941_film) "Blood and Sand (1941 film)")* (1941) – technical adviser
* *Military Training* (1941) (short) – assistant director
* *[Submarine Raider](/wiki/Submarine_Raider "Submarine Raider")* (1942) – uncredited director
